content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Trina Coffinan-Gomez 
City Councilmember, Dist. 6 
Administration Building 
275 Main Street, Suite 400 
Watsonville, Ca 95076 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-006 
Dear Ms. Coffman-Gomez: 
March 25, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn (the "Act")1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict 
of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as 
common law conflict of interest or Section I 090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts 
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders 
advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you, a mortgage broker, from making or 
participating in making city council decisions to (i) approve a city budget that contains an allocation 
for the city's affordable housing program (the "Program"), or (ii) any other decisions related to the 
funding of the Program? 
CONCLUSION 
No. You do not have a disqualifying conflict of interest because the governmental decisions 
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your business. 
FACTS 
You are a member of the Watsonville city council, and are also a mortgage broker and real 
estate broker operating as a sole proprietorship. As a mortgage broker, you assist clients in 
obtaining loans, including loans from the city under the Program. However, you are not 
compensated for assisting a client with a loan under the Program. Because the program only 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-006 
Page No. 2 
provides a portion of the financing, you receive compensation only for assisting the client in 
obtaining financing through a primary lender for the balance of the purchase not financed through 
'l 
the Program.-
The United States Department of Housing and Urban Development provides funds to the 
California Department of Housing and Community Development, which are used to fund loans for 
eligible, low-income home buyers. The city, which applies for and receives such funds on a regular 
basis, in essence acts as a conduit for distributing the federal monies to low-income buyers within 
the city. 
The city council does not set criteria for loan eligibility, discuss funds available through the 
Program or vote on a buyer's eligibility for the Program. The city council does, however, routinely 
approve and adopt eligibility standards set by state guidelines. City staff process and review home 
buyers for income and property eligibility using the state's criteria. 
In addition, the city council annually adopts a city budget which includes a comprehensive 
financial report on the city's overall financial health. In adopting the budget, the city council is 
accepting the report's findings which includes an allocation of funds for the Program. However, 
there are no specific votes relating to the funds. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 
and include the following: 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
Foreseeability: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard 
for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. 
Under your facts, your business is not explicitly involved in decisions regarding the Program. 
Thus, as applied to your business, Regulation 1870l{b) provides: "In general, if the financial effect 
can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably 
H We note that the Act also prohibits you from attempting to use your positon to influence a governmental 
decision. For purposes of obtaining a loan through the city, any contact with a city official is an attempt to use your 
positon to influence the decision. While the Act does not prohibit you from assisting a client with completing an 
application, we do not analyze any communication with the city you may have in securing a loan for a client including 
the use of your name in submitting an application. 


File No. A-16-006 
Page No. 3 
foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not 
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." 
Materiality: Under the materiality standard for business entities, the reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect of a governmental decision is material if "a prudent person ... would find it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price 
of the businesses entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately held business entity." 
(Regulations 18702.1 (b) and 18702.3(a)(4).) 
You state that you are not compensated for arranging a loan under the Program for your 
clients. Any fees you earn for arranging mortgage loans are paid solely by the primary lender for 
arranging the primary loan. While there may be minimal indirect impacts on your business resulting 
from, for example, a potential increase in the number of homes sold and from which you might 
receive additional business from primary lenders, there is no indication that any of the decisions are 
of a magnitude that will affect the value of your business. Therefore, you do not have a conflict of 
interest in the decisions.3 
VJ:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division 
3 Because you receive commission income as a mortgage and real estate broker, you may also have potentially 
disqualifying interests in any person you represent, any person represented by an agent working under your auspices, 
any brokerage business through which you conduct your business, and any person who receives a finder's or referral fee 
for referring the transaction to you or makes a referral pursuant to a contract with you. (Regulation 18700. l(c)(2)(B).) 
However, absent additional facts, we similarly find no reasonably foreseeable material effect on any of these potential 
interests based upon a general decision regarding the funding of the Program.

QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you, a mortgage broker, from making or 
participating in making city council decisions to (i) approve a city budget that contains an allocation 
for the city's affordable housing program (the "Program"), or (ii) any other decisions related to the 
funding of the Program?

CONCLUSION: No. You do not have a disqualifying conflict of interest because the governmental decisions 
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your business.
